content_html:
Synopsis

Two men driven to tell the truth … whatever the cost.

A research chemist comes under personal and professional attack when he decides to appear in a 60 Minutes exposé on Big Tobacco.

Directed by Michael Mann

A research chemist comes under personal and professional attack when he decides to appear in a 60 Minutes exposé on Big Tobacco.

A bennfentes, Révélations, O Informante, Вътрешен човек, Sisäpiirissä, Probuđena savjest, El dilema, Ha-Makkor, Dietro la verità, Scandal în industria tutunului, Köstebek, 인사이더, インサイダー, 奪命煙幕, nsider: Muž, který věděl příliš mnoho, Insider, نفوذی (افشاگر), Insider - Dietro la verità, O Informador, Свой человек, 惊曝内幕, Insider: Muž, který věděl příliš mnoho, Informator, המקור, Своя людина, Muž, ktorý vedel priveľa, Informatorius, 驚爆內幕, El Informante, المطلع, Nội Gián, คดีโลกตะลึง, The insider - Sisäpiirissä

Ordinary people under extraordinary pressure, Mike. What the hell do you expect? Grace and consistency?

"Maybe things have changed."
"What's changed?"
"You mean since this morning?"
"No, I mean since whenever."

Corporate interests have never (and will never) not come at the expense of our own, and here Mann richly dramatizes the crushing pressures, lasting damages, and sheer difficulty inherent to resisting them. Are we running out of heroes or are they being systematically broken down? Is there even anything left for them to save? God Crowe, God Pacino, God Plummer.

"No, you fucked you. Don't invert stuff." When we realized we'd corporatized everything. Analog's last stand.

Thrilling, beautiful, moving, a modern masterpiece about the real heroes who made cigarettes even better and funner to smoke.

When Russell Crowe goes and looks out at the water to contemplate his life? Yeah that’s the good shit

it's impossible to be a person of integrity and exist within the highest-echelons of modern systems; at that level, every scientist has made ethical compromises for the sake of profits and convenience, every journalist has lied to the public and spread disinformation. you don't get to live the life of luxury without screwing someone over who never deserved it, because it's those sins that put you into the debt of rich people, rich people who will leverage that debt against you should you ever move against them. you sin, they own you, simple as that.

the only way to preserve your ethics is to get the fuck out while you still can, to hold fast against the social and economic…

I actually just rewatched all of Christopher Plummer’s scenes, which comprise The Greatest Acting of All Time.

al pacino is my husband... I would do anything for him... my taurus king... the love of my life

Drowned by corporatised America and the judicial ramifications that accompany it. an expose on modern-day institutions and the financial ladder - the lengths they will go to silence people in the know, as well as the absence of integrity displayed by those who have reached the very top of the pyramid. A hazardous and endless cycle. A film with everlasting relevance. Mann has a talent for making any conversation the most engaging fucking thing ever, and the entire 160 minutes just flew by. An acting masterclass from everyone involved. Even my fiancée, whose taste in films is not typically this kind, absolutely adored it. We should save the term "American epic" strictly for films like this, if we're being completely honest. Some film.

summary: All this Sturm und Drang surrounding 60 Minutes has me thinking about a re-watch of The Insider , Michael Mann’s great 1999 movie. Letterboxd’s synopsis: “A research chemist comes under personal and professional attack when he decides to appear in a 60 Minutes exposé on Big Tobacco.” It’s a great movie, and feels apt AF at the moment. Here’s the original se…
title: ‘The Insider’
